grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: General HSAB principle: Hg2+ (soft acid) tends to prefer soft sulfur donors. SRD-46 outputs shown here do not state an across-donor-type ranking for Hg2+.
This ordering (S > N > O) is general HSAB reasoning; the provided SRD-46 rows do not explicitly rank Hg2+ binding by donor type.

paragraphs.text:
| Observation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Very strong Hg²⁺ complexes appear repeatedly with sulfur ligands | consistent with **thiophilic** behavior |
| Typical N-donor ligands are strong to moderate, but usually below the strongest S donors | N binds well, but less preferentially than S overall |
| Simple O-donor ligands are weak or even unfavorable as simple 1:1 HgL species | O donors are generally least preferred |

claims.text: | Very strong Hg²⁺ complexes appear repeatedly with sulfur ligands | consistent with **thiophilic** behavior |
| Typical N-donor ligands are strong to moderate, but usually below the strongest S donors | N binds well, but less preferentially than S overall |
| Simple O-donor ligands are weak or even unfavorable as simple 1:1 HgL species | O donors are generally least preferred |

paragraphs.text: One caveat: **ethylenediamine** is unusually strong for an N donor because it is a **chelating bidentate ligand**, so its log K = 14.3 does not overturn the overall trend. Likewise, **EDTA** is strong because of multidentate chelation, not because Hg²⁺ intrinsically prefers oxygen donors.

claims.text: **ethylenediamine** is unusually strong for an N donor
does not overturn the overall trend.
not because Hg²⁺ intrinsically prefers oxygen donors.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Donor preference order is not listed in SRD-46 rows; this reflects HSAB: Hg(II) (soft acid) favors S donors. E.g., thiourea ML2 logK 22.1 vs carbonate ML logK -11.
Not directly stated in SRD-46; consistent with HSAB trend S > N > O. N example stronger than O: en ML2 logK 23.19 vs carbonate ML2 14.5.
Preference order isn’t given in SRD-46; HSAB implies O donors weakest for Hg(II). Carbonate examples: ML logK -11, ML2 14.5.

paragraphs.text:
Chemically, this fits HSAB ideas: **Hg²⁺ is a soft acid**, so it tends to bind **soft sulfur bases** especially strongly.  
Source: **NIST SRD-46**.

claims.text: it tends to bind **soft sulfur bases** especially strongly.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: HSAB is standard chemistry; SRD-46 rows list constants but do not state HSAB explicitly. The Hg2+–S ligand constants below are consistent with HSAB.
Hg2+ being a soft Lewis acid is established HSAB knowledge; SRD-46 tool outputs do not label metals as ‘soft’ or ‘hard’.
Tool results in this run are from NIST Standard Reference Database 46 (SRD-46); provenance applies though not repeated in the row outputs.
